abridged.title: Abridged Snapshot
abridged.content:
Bedside purpose

Derived haemodynamic parameters convert raw pressures and cardiac output into clinically useful patterns. In CT-ICU they help identify cardiogenic shock, hypovolaemia, vasoplegia, RV failure, pulmonary hypertension and tamponade. They should be interpreted as patterns, not isolated numbers.

Parameter Formula / unit Usual range
Cardiac index (CI) CO / BSA, L/min/m² 2.5-4.0
Stroke volume (SV) CO / HR x 1000, mL/beat 60-100 mL/beat
SVR (MAP - CVP) / CO x 80, dyn·s/cm5 800-1200
PVR (mPAP - PAOP) / CO x 80, dyn·s/cm5 <250, often 20-130
MAP DBP + 1/3 pulse pressure, mmHg 65-100 depending target

A normal MAP can coexist with dangerous low cardiac output if SVR is high. This is why cold, oliguric patients with a MAP of 70 still need escalation.

expanded.title: Expanded Deep-Dive
expanded.content:
Core calculations

Cardiac index — Cardiac output adjusted for body surface area. It is the central flow number in many CT-ICU haemodynamic assessments.

Stroke volume — The amount of blood ejected per beat. It falls with poor preload, poor contractility, excessive afterload, tamponade, severe valve dysfunction or RV failure.

Systemic vascular resistance — The calculated resistance the LV/systemic circulation works against. It rises with vasoconstriction and falls with vasoplegia.

Pulmonary vascular resistance — The calculated resistance the RV pumps against. It rises with hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidosis, pulmonary hypertension, PE, high airway pressure and pulmonary vasoconstriction.

SVR is calculated from pressure gradient divided by flow: (MAP - CVP) / CO x 80. PVR is calculated as (mean PA pressure - PAOP) / CO x 80. These formulae show why resistance can appear high when flow is very low, and why calculated values must be interpreted with the patient condition.

Shock patterns

Pattern CI SVR CVP/PAOP Typical CT-ICU causes
Cardiogenic shock Low <2.2 High or normal PAOP often high if LV failure; CVP high if RV failure LV failure after bypass, myocardial stunning, graft/ischaemia, valve dysfunction, arrhythmia.
Hypovolaemic shock Low High CVP/PAOP low or falling Bleeding, vasodilation with inadequate volume, diuresis, third spacing.
Vasoplegic shock Normal/high or low-normal Low <800 Filling pressures variable Post-CPB inflammatory vasodilation, sepsis, ACE inhibitor effect.
Obstructive/tamponade Low High CVP high, PAOP may equalise Tamponade/clot, tension pneumothorax, massive PE, severe RV afterload.
RV failure Low or low-normal Variable CVP high, PA pressures/PVR high, PAOP normal/low unless LV disease Pulmonary hypertension, hypoxia, acidosis, protamine reaction, RV infarct, high PEEP.

Stroke work indices

RVSWI and LVSWI estimate ventricular work per beat adjusted for body surface area. LVSWI falls with LV systolic failure or low filling. RVSWI falls in RV failure and can be misleadingly strained when PVR is high. These are advanced trend tools rather than single-decision values.

pearls.title: Clinical Pearls
pearls.content:
MAP is pressure, not flow. A clamped-down patient can have a reasonable MAP and terrible cardiac output.

Vasoplegia after bypass often has low diastolic pressure, warm peripheries and high vasopressor requirement. The treatment focus is vascular tone, not just fluid.

High CVP with low CI and high PVR is RV danger territory. Avoid hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidosis and excessive airway pressure.

Do not calculate SVR/PVR from bad inputs. A wrong wedge, wrong CVP level or inaccurate CO makes the derived value wrong with mathematical confidence.

equipment.title: Equipment Tie-In
equipment.content:
Equipment tie-in

Derived parameters are only as reliable as their inputs. The monitor may calculate CI, SVR and PVR automatically, but it requires correct height/weight or BSA, accurate CO, correct pressure transducer levelling, valid wedge and correct channel mapping.

targets.title: Targets and Numbers
targets.content:
Parameter Usual value Actionable interpretation
CI 2.5-4.0 L/min/m² <2.2 with hypoperfusion = low cardiac output syndrome until proven otherwise.
SVI 35-65 mL/beat/m² Low SVI with high HR can hide poor beat-to-beat output.
SVR 800-1200 dyn·s/cm5 <800 suggests vasoplegia; >1200 suggests vasoconstriction/compensation or excessive pressor.
PVR <250 dyn·s/cm5 High PVR strains RV; correct hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidosis and excessive airway pressure.
PAOP 6-12 mmHg High suggests LV filling/pulmonary congestion if valid; low may support hypovolaemia.
CVP 2-8 mmHg; ventilated often 5-12 High with low CI suggests RV failure/obstruction; low with low CI may be hypovolaemia.

quiz.question: A patient has MAP 75, CI 1.7, cool peripheries, lactate rising and SVR high. What is the main problem?
options: Adequate perfusion because MAP is normal
Low cardiac output with compensatory vasoconstriction
quiz.explanation: Pressure is preserved by high SVR, but flow is inadequate. CI and perfusion markers indicate low cardiac output.

quiz.question: What is the formula for SVR?
quiz.explanation: SVR is systemic pressure gradient divided by flow multiplied by 80.

quiz.question: Which pattern best fits vasoplegia after bypass?
options: Low SVR, low diastolic pressure, warm peripheries, vasopressor need
High SVR and cold peripheries always
High wedge and pulmonary oedema only
quiz.explanation: Vasoplegia is loss of vascular tone after CPB/inflammation, producing low SVR and often low diastolic pressure.
